metadata.subject: Career Education
metadata.description: Career-Life Education is a required 4-credit course for all BC students, typically completed in Grade 10. Students engage in career-life exploration by examining the influences on their career-life choices, connecting with mentors and community networks, developing their personal profiles and digital footprints, and beginning to plan their career-life journeys. A focus on well-being, balance, and reciprocal community relationships positions career planning as a whole-life practice. First Peoples perspectives on community contribution, relational knowing, and non-linear career paths are integrated throughout.
metadata.general_description: A required 4-credit career education course developing career-life planning skills through self-reflection, mentorship, community connection, digital literacy, and the development of flexible career-life plans — foundational preparation for Career-Life Connections.

big_ideas.statement: Career-life choices are made in a recurring cycle of planning, reflecting, adapting, and deciding.
big_ideas.elaboration: Career planning is not a single event — it is an ongoing, iterative process of gathering information, setting goals, trying new experiences, reflecting on what worked, and adapting plans accordingly. This cyclical model applies throughout a person's life.

big_ideas.statement: Career-life decisions are influenced by internal and external factors, including local and global trends.
big_ideas.elaboration: Personal values, interests, and strengths are internal factors that guide career choices. External factors — economic conditions, labour market trends, family expectations, geography, and social context — also shape opportunities and constraints. Understanding both enables more informed decision-making.

big_ideas.statement: Cultivating networks and reciprocal relationships with family, peers, and community members can support and broaden career-life awareness and options.
big_ideas.elaboration: The people we know — mentors, family, employers, educators, and community members — are among the most important resources in career-life development. Building and maintaining these relationships reciprocally is both a practical strategy and an ethical stance.

big_ideas.statement: Finding balance between personal and work life promotes well-being.
big_ideas.elaboration: Sustainable career-life development requires attention to physical, emotional, social, and spiritual well-being. Understanding how work integrates with — rather than displaces — other life values supports long-term health and satisfaction.

In a rapidly changing world, the willingness and capacity to keep learning — through formal education, on-the-job experience, mentorship, and self-directed inquiry — is one of the most valuable career assets a person can develop.

competencies.raw: Examine the influences of personal and public profiles on career-life opportunities; examine the risks and benefits associated with personal and public digital footprints; examine the role of personal and employment networks in exploring opportunities.
i_can_statements: I can examine how my personal profile — strengths, interests, values, and identity — influences my career-life opportunities.
I can analyze the risks and benefits of my digital footprint and manage my public online presence intentionally.
I can recognize the role that personal networks — family, peers, mentors, and community connections — play in opening career-life doors.

competencies.raw: Apply a mentor's guidance in career-life exploration; collaborate with supportive community members to explore reciprocal influences of career-life choices; communicate to highlight personal strengths, talents, accomplishments, and abilities.
i_can_statements: I can identify and connect with a mentor, apply their guidance to my career-life exploration, and reflect on what I've learned.
I can collaborate with community members in ways that are reciprocal — contributing as well as receiving support.
I can communicate my strengths, talents, and accomplishments effectively in verbal, written, and digital contexts.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate inclusive, respectful, and safe interactions in diverse career-life environments; identify challenges and opportunities and generate and apply strategies; explore and connect experiential learning with possible career-life pathways; practise effective strategies for healthy school/work/life balance.
i_can_statements: I can demonstrate inclusive, respectful, and safe interactions across diverse community, volunteer, and workplace settings.
I can identify challenges and opportunities in my career-life exploration and generate strategies to navigate them.
I can reflect on real experiences — volunteering, job shadowing, projects — and connect them to possible future pathways.
I can practise and reflect on strategies for maintaining healthy balance between school, work, and personal life.

competencies.raw: Explore and reflect on career-life roles and personal growth; develop preliminary profiles and flexible plans for career-life learning journeys.
i_can_statements: I can explore diverse career-life roles and reflect on how each connects to my values, strengths, and goals.
I can develop a preliminary personal profile and a flexible, draft plan for my career-life learning journey.
I can articulate my preliminary career-life direction and the steps I plan to take toward it.

areas.name: Career-Life Development
areas.raw: Mentorship, well-being, self-assessment, personal representation, workplace safety.
topics: Mentorship: identifying mentors, establishing mentoring relationships, and applying guidance
Well-being strategies: stress management, physical health, social connection, and work-life balance
Preferred learning methods: identifying how you learn best and applying this to career exploration
Core Competencies: communication, thinking, personal and social responsibility
Self-assessment: inventories for interests, skills, values, and personality
Personal representation: resumes, cover letters, portfolios, and professional communication
Workplace behaviour: professionalism, punctuality, teamwork, and workplace culture
Safety in the workplace: WHMIS, WorkSafe BC, young worker rights, and hazard identification

areas.name: Connections with Community
areas.raw: Inclusive practices, networking, employment marketing, community contribution, volunteering.
topics: Inclusive practices: equity, diversity, and respect in the workplace and community
Networking strategies: building and maintaining professional and community relationships
Employment marketing: understanding how to present yourself to employers and post-secondary institutions
Personal, environmental, and land use factors: how geography and environment shape local economies
First Peoples community contribution: traditional knowledge, land stewardship, and community roles
Community contribution: volunteering, service learning, and reciprocal community relationships
Exploring diverse work environments: job shadowing, informational interviews, and field visits

areas.name: Career-Life Planning
areas.raw: Research on diverse roles, portfolio organization, decision-making, financial planning, labour market trends.
topics: Researching diverse career-life roles: career clusters, occupational profiles, and non-traditional paths
Labour market trends: in-demand sectors, automation, and the green economy
Portfolio organization: collecting and curating evidence of skills, achievements, and experiences
Decision-making models: rational, intuitive, and values-based decision-making frameworks
Financial planning: budgeting, savings, income planning, and education costs
Post-graduation opportunities: college, university, trades, apprenticeship, and work
BC graduation requirements: credits, Dogwood Certificate, and Graduation Transitions
Developing a preliminary career-life plan: goals, timelines, and flexible next steps
